annotator S.report_text:
CosmicDuke infections start by tricking victims into opening either a PDF file that contains an exploit or a Windows executable whose filename is manipulated to make it look like a document or image file. An adversary may rely upon a user opening a malicious file in order to gain execution. Adversaries may attempt to manipulate features of their artifacts to make them appear legitimate or benign to users and/or security tools.
keylogger Adversaries may use keylogging to capture keystrokes to collect credentials.
clipboard stealer Theft of data in the system clipboard.
screenshotter, Capture of desktop screens or specific windows
password stealers
It also collects information about the files on the system Adversaries may search local system sources, such as file systems and configuration files or local databases, to find files of interest and sensitive data prior to Exfiltration.
has the capability to export cryptographic certificates and the associated private keys. Adversaries may search compromised systems to find and obtain insecurely stored credentials.
it is sent out to remote servers via FTP Adversaries may steal data by exfiltrating it over a different protocol than that of the existing command and control channel.
Cosmu allows the attacker to download and execute other malware on the system Adversaries may transfer tools or other files from an external system into a compromised environment.
It is possible that the PDF documents containing exploits were emailed to the targeted users as file attachments. Adversaries may send spearphishing emails with a malicious attachment in an attempt to gain access to victim systems.
CosmicDuke samples use simple social engineering to trick the user into willingly launching the attack file.
the malware's executable file is first disguised as an image or document to make it seem innocuous.
Keylogger Adversaries may use keylogging to capture keystrokes to collect credentials.
Taking screenshots Adversaries may use screen capture to collect information by capturing the screen content.
Stealing data from clipboard Adversaries may collect data stored in the clipboard.
Stealing files Adversaries may search local system sources, such as file systems and configuration files or local databases, to find files of interest and sensitive data prior to Exfiltration.
Stealing PKI certificates and associated private keys Adversaries may search compromised systems to find and obtain insecurely stored credentials.
Stealing usernames and passwords from browsers, instant messengers and email clients y StealingWLAN passwords y StealingWindows password hashes Adversaries may search for common password storage locations to obtain user credentials.
The information collected by the malware is automatically uploaded to remote servers via FTP.
It creates a scheduled task and installs a Windows service. Adversaries may abuse the Windows Task Scheduler to perform task scheduling for initial or recurring execution of malicious code. Adversaries may create or modify Windows services to repeatedly execute malicious payloads as part of persistence.
The malware uses the FTP servers and WebDav servers both for exfiltrating the collected data and for updating the malware.
Cosmu uses RC4 to decrypt incoming data and encrypt outgoing data. Adversaries may encode data with a standard data encoding system to make the content of command and control traffic more difficult to detect.

annotator L.report_text:
MiniDuke

or

a Windows executable whose filename is manipulated to make it look like a document or image file. This technique involves inducing the user to run a malicious file, such as a Windows executable whose name has been manipulated to look like a document or image file.

keylogger, recording of keystrokes made by the user to capture sensitive information such as credentials.

clipboard stealer, Theft of data in the system clipboard.

screenshotter, Capture of desktop screens or specific windows

password stealers

It also collects information about the files on the system Collection of data from files in the system.

the capability to export cryptographic certificates and the associated private keys The exfiltration of private keys and cryptographic certificates.

it is sent out to remote servers via FTP Use of FTP specifically to exfiltrate data

Cosmu allows the attacker to download and execute other malware on the system Adversaries may transfer tools or other files from an external system into a compromised environment.

CosmicDuke infections start by tricking victims into opening either a PDF file that contains an exploit Exploitation of vulnerabilities in client software to execute code. In the case of CosmicDuke, opening a PDF containing an exploit exploits a vulnerability to execute malicious code.

Once the victim opens the file, the malware gains persistence on the system and starts collecting information.

Once the information has been collected

Stealing usernames and passwords from browsers, instant messengers and email clients

The information collected by the malware is automatically uploaded to remote servers via FTP Use of FTP specifically to exfiltrate data

It is possible that the PDF documents containing exploits were emailed to the targeted users as file attachments Sending malicious attachments via email to induce victims to open them. PDF documents containing exploits are sent to targeted users as email attachments.

CosmicDuke malware samples that use exploits to gain entry onto a target system start with a malicious Flash object embedded into a PDF file. Exploitation of known vulnerabilities in publicly exposed applications, as in the case of exploits used in PDF files containing malicious Flash objects

When the file is launched, the object exploits the known CVE-2011-0611 vulnerability in specific versions of Adobe Flash, Reader and Acrobat products. exploitation of vulnerabilities in client software to execute malicious code. the malicious Flash object embedded in the PDF file exploits a known vulnerability in Adobe Flash, Reader, and Acrobat to execute the malware.

social engineering to trick the user into willingly launching the attack file CosmicDuke uses social engineering to induce the user to launch the attack file.

the malware's executable file is first disguised as an image or document to make it seem innocuous Disguising the malicious file as an image or document to look legitimate.

Keylogger recording of keystrokes made by the user to capture sensitive information such as credentials.

Taking screenshots Capture of desktop screens or specific windows

Stealing data from clipboard Theft of data in the system clipboard.

Stealing files Collection of data from files in the system.

Stealing PKI certificates and associated private keys The exfiltration of private keys and cryptographic certificates.

It creates a scheduled task and installs a Windows service Creation of planned activities to maintain persistence on the system.

Cosmu uses RC4 to decrypt incoming data and encrypt outgoing data use of standard encodings to transform data for transfer or storage, as in the case of using RC4 to encrypt and decrypt data.

The malware uses the FTP servers and WebDav servers both for exfiltrating the collected data and for updating the malware Adversaries may steal data by exfiltrating it over a different protocol than that of the existing command and control channel.
